story:
They say the road to hell is paved with good intentions. Ours was, too. A good idea, pushed too fast, became the thing that broke the world. My father, a climatologist with a physicist’s caution, warned them. He worked on the program, then begged them for more time. He said the atmosphere and the living world were too complex, that the technology could behave in ways no one expected. But people listened to politicians over scientists. With carbon rising and storms, floods, fires, and heat turning life into a countdown, the world demanded a quick fix.

The fix was elegant and terrifying: carbon-eating synthetic nanobots, released high in the atmosphere, programmed to latch onto airborne carbon compounds and neutralize them. A cloud of them, in their zillions. The first launch felt like a global holiday. Crowds cheered in London, Sydney, New York, Tokyo. They told us the sky wouldn’t be blue anymore, that it would tint violet. It did. We learned to live under that strange, beautiful sky as the rockets kept going up. Carbon levels fell. The price was high, and the effort constant.

Then came 2.0: self-replicating nanobots, meant to copy themselves for up to ten years without the weekly launches. Cheaper. Smarter. Final. My father had been pushed out by then—no dissent allowed. I remember his silence as the first 2.0 rocket rose through the violet light, the way he pulled me close and kissed my forehead. He didn’t say I told you so. He didn’t need to.

2.0 worked—too well. It replicated, and because it could rewrite its own instructions, it evolved. It drifted down into the lower atmosphere, and then the rain came. Violet rain. The droplets carried nanobots that no longer distinguished between atmospheric carbon and the carbon that holds life together. They stripped flesh, crops, topsoil, plastics, fuel, fabrics—anything rich in reactive carbon—while leaving steel frames, concrete shells, and glass facades standing like picked bones. Central Europe was hit first. Twenty-five thousand people vanished in the first hour. Hundreds of miles of countryside were scraped to bare stone. Panic surged. Politicians blamed scientists. Scientists blamed politicians. The rain kept falling. Whole neighborhoods died in minutes, their streets choked with empty buildings and burned-out cars. The nanobots kept copying. The violet sky darkened.

Every forecast felt like Russian roulette. Every drop on your skin could be death. My father sent me with Uncle Greg to an old research base in the Nevada mountains, a place he used when he studied rainfall patterns. We went deep underground by a rickety elevator, all ropes and gears, its motor coughing like a lawn mower. I begged him to come with us, or let me stay. He smiled sadly and said I was like Mom—headstrong. He promised he’d follow in three days. It’s been twelve. We have canned food and water from a sealed artesian tank the base drew years ago from a deep aquifer, still clean behind ceramic valves. We have air—the old filters still work. We have each other. And above us the rain has been steady for five days now.

When Greg snores, I ride the elevator to Level 2 and listen. The rhythm of rain on stone pulls me back to my one bright memory of Mom, the summer day she and Dad laughed and danced in a sudden shower, beckoning me with outstretched hands. I don’t remember if I joined them. I dream I did. In my dream we’re together again, laughing, spinning under a sky we can’t fix, dancing in the violet rain.
